questions.q: State the decision rule for choosing between RPA and BPA, using the keyword pair from the episode.
options: Automate the task with RPA, or rethink the process with BPA — the deciding keyword is task versus process
Use RPA for cheap projects and BPA for expensive projects, because cost is the deciding factor
Use RPA for new systems and BPA for legacy systems, because age decides the approach
Automate the front end with RPA and the back end with BPA, depending on the layer involved
questions.explanation: The episode's core decision rule is: automate the task with RPA, or rethink the process with BPA — RPA is a Robot doing a Task, BPA is redesigning a Process. The deciding keyword is task versus process. Cost (option A) is a secondary nuance — RPA is the quick tactical patch and BPA the strategic fix — but it is not the keyword that decides the approach.

questions.q: Identify what the headline word BRITTLE captures about robotic process automation.
options: RPA bots are physically fragile robots that wear out and need hardware replacement
RPA breaks when the user interface changes, because the bot mimics a human reading a screen rather than talking to a stable API
RPA bots are slow to run and cannot keep up with high transaction volumes
RPA breaks because it cannot handle encrypted data passing between systems
questions.explanation: BRITTLE is the memory hook for RPA's central maintainability risk: the bot works through the user interface — finding fields and buttons by position or label — so the moment a layout, field name, or step changes, it fails. Option A misreads 'brittle' as physical hardware; RPA is software mimicking a human at the screen, and screens change, unlike a stable back-end API.

questions.q: Outline how robotic process automation is typically implemented, according to the episode.
options: Through deep integration with system databases and APIs, custom-developed with a workflow engine
By rewriting the legacy application's source code to add automated routines
On low-code platforms with visual workflow designers, using bots that read the screen and simulate keyboard and mouse input without changing the underlying applications
Through a cloud service that replaces both the user interface and the back-end systems
questions.explanation: RPA is typically built on low-code platforms with drag-and-drop visual designers, using bots that read the screen and simulate keyboard and mouse input — sitting on top of existing interfaces with no changes to the underlying applications, which is why it suits legacy systems. Option A describes BPA's deep integration and workflow engine, not RPA.

questions.q: Explain why working through the user interface is precisely what makes RPA brittle.
options: The user interface is encrypted, so the bot cannot read it reliably and crashes intermittently
The user interface runs on the server, so network latency causes the bot to time out

Because the bot locates fields and buttons by their position or label the way a human would, any update that moves a button, renames a field, or changes the layout means the bot can no longer find what it expects and fails
Because the bot stores its data in the user interface, which is wiped each time the page reloads
questions.explanation: An RPA bot depends entirely on the screen staying the same: it finds fields and buttons by position or label, so a moved button, renamed field, or changed layout breaks it. This is why RPA drives the volatile Front of Front-Back-Store, whereas a real integration through the Back via an API is far more stable. The encryption option invents a problem that the episode never raises.

questions.q: Outline two security considerations that must be applied when deploying an automated bot.
options: Encrypt the bot's source code and run it only on weekends to limit exposure
Give the bot full administrator access so it never fails, and disable input validation for speed
Store the bot's passwords inside its workflow file and share them with all staff for transparency
Protect the bot's credentials (never hard-code keys; use a secrets manager) and apply least privilege so the bot has only the permissions it needs
questions.explanation: A bot acts on systems using credentials, so those credentials must never be hard-coded and should be kept in a secrets manager, and the bot — being a privileged account — must be given least privilege, only the permissions it needs. Option C inverts both controls; least privilege exists precisely to limit damage if the bot is compromised. Validating inputs (VSE) and minding data privacy (PER) are further valid considerations.

questions.q: Distinguish between how RPA and BPA are built.
options: RPA uses deep database integration while BPA screen-scrapes the user interface

RPA sits on top of existing interfaces using the UI without changing the systems, while BPA uses deep integration through proper interfaces and databases with a workflow engine orchestrating approvals
Both are built identically; the only difference is the name given to the project
RPA requires a custom workflow engine and audit trail, while BPA is a simple low-code drag-and-drop tool
questions.explanation: RPA is low-code and works on top of existing user interfaces without modifying the systems, which is why it suits legacy applications; BPA connects systems through their proper interfaces and databases with a workflow engine routing approvals and maintaining an audit trail. Option D reverses the two — the workflow engine and audit trail belong to BPA, and drag-and-drop low-code is the RPA hallmark.

questions.q: Compare RPA and BPA as strategic choices for an organisation.
options: RPA is the strategic fix that treats the cause; BPA is the tactical patch that treats the symptom
Both are tactical patches that only ever treat symptoms, never the underlying cause
RPA is the quick, low-code tactical patch that treats the symptom; BPA is the slower, more expensive strategic fix that redesigns the process and treats the cause
RPA and BPA are equally fast and costly, so the choice makes no strategic difference
questions.explanation: The episode frames RPA as the quick win — fast, low-code, no system changes — but a tactical patch that treats the symptom, while BPA is the strategic fix: slower and more expensive, but it actually improves the process by redesigning it, treating the cause. Option A swaps the two; RPA never re-engineers the process, so it cannot be the cause-treating strategic fix.

questions.q: A company's staff manually copy data between two old systems that cannot easily be modified, and they want it done faster without changing either system. Which approach fits, and what is the main risk to flag?
options: RPA, because it is one repetitive task on unmodifiable systems with no process change wanted; the main risk is brittleness, since any future interface update will break the bot
BPA, because copying data is a whole process; the main risk is the high upfront cost of redesign
BPA, because the systems are old; the main risk is that the workflow engine will be incompatible
RPA, because it is cheap; there is no real risk since the bot does not touch the systems themselves
questions.explanation: The tells point to RPA — one repetitive task, systems that cannot be modified, no process change wanted — so a bot that sits on top of the existing interfaces is the right tool. The strong, two-part answer also flags brittleness: because the bot reads those screens, any interface update breaks it, so it needs ongoing maintenance and is not a permanent fix. The final option falls into the trap of treating RPA as a permanent, risk-free solution.

questions.q: A bank wants to automate its loan-application process, which currently spans four separate systems and involves several manual approvals. Recommend RPA or BPA and justify the choice.
options: RPA, because it is fast and cheap to bolt bots onto each of the four existing systems
RPA, because four interfaces are easier for a bot to screen-scrape than one large integration
Either approach works equally well, since the number of systems does not affect the decision
BPA, because the process spans four systems and several approvals — a whole end-to-end process best redesigned with proper integration, a workflow engine routing approvals, and an audit trail
questions.explanation: Because the loan process spans four systems and several manual approvals, it is a whole end-to-end process rather than a single repetitive task, so BPA — with deep integration, a workflow engine routing approvals, and an audit trail — is the sustainable choice despite higher upfront cost. The first option is the trap: RPA would only bolt brittle bots onto four changing interfaces and would not address the underlying inefficiency.

questions.q: In an 'Evaluate the use of RPA' extended-response question, which structure earns the full marks?
options: A list of benefits only, since RPA is recommended for repetitive tasks
Benefits, then costs and risks (naming brittleness, credential security, and employment impact), then a justified conditional judgement
A one-sided judgement stating 'RPA is good because it saves time', with no downsides
Costs and risks only, concluding that RPA should never be used
questions.explanation: An evaluate answer must weigh both sides then land a judgement: benefits (quick, low-code, frees staff), then costs and risks (brittleness named with its cause, credentials secured under least privilege, employment and reskilling impact), then a justified conditional judgement. Option C is the explicit weak, band-four trap — one-sided, naming nothing, no judgement — that the episode warns against.
